Client 1: Ballpark, like one-thirty-ish a year.
Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m just noting that. Patricia, do you have the more exact annual gross number you use?
Client 2: Yes. Household gross income is 132300 per year.
Advisor: Got it. And on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run—everything living, bills, travel, the normal stuff?
Client 2: Total monthly expenses are 6050.

Client 2: Yes. Household gross income is 132300 per year.
Advisor: Got it. And on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run—everything living, bills, travel, the normal stuff?
Client 2: Total monthly expenses are 6050.
Advisor: Okay. Does that include the bigger irregular items too, or is that more the steady monthly burn?
Client 2: That’s the steady monthly burn. The irregular stuff we handle as it comes, but 6050 is the baseline.

Client 1: Ballpark, like one-thirty-ish a year.

Client 1: No. Monthly debt payments total is 0.
Advisor: Great—so no loans outstanding. That simplifies things. Let’s shift to what you’ve built up. When you add up the investable accounts—so not the house—what’s the total investable assets number you track?
Client 2: Investable assets total is 783150.
Advisor: Thank you. We’ll break that into the actual accounts so I understand what’s where. Patricia, I have you as holding a taxable account at a bank—does that sound right? What’s the value on that one?
Client 2: Yes, that’s mine. It’s 388000.

Client 1: 117500.
Advisor: Okay. And then separate from investable assets, you’ve got your primary residence—what do you estimate the home value at right now?
Client 2: We have it at 582650.
Advisor: Great. Let me pause and make sure I’m not missing any income sources before we talk protection. Daniel, your income—how does it come in? Is it mostly business salary plus some rental?
Client 1: Yeah. I pay myself a salary and we’ve got rental income monthly.

Client 1: Thanks. Honestly, a clear plan—like, what we do next and when. I don’t want this to drag on forever.
Client 2: And I want the numbers to line up. If we’re making decisions, I’d like them grounded in what’s actually coming in and going out.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start with cash flow at the household level, then we’ll hit assets, and I’ll ask about any debts and insurance. Daniel, what are you seeing for total gross income for the household in a year—ballpark is fine, then we’ll pin it down.
Client 1: Ballpark, like one-thirty-ish a year.
Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m just noting that. Patricia, do you have the more exact annual gross number you use?

Client 2: Yes, we’re in the US.
Advisor: Great. And on taxes—Patricia, you’d mentioned you’re in a pretty high bracket. What bracket are you planning around right now?
Client 2: We’re planning around the 37% bracket.
Advisor: Got it. Daniel, when you think about investing style—how much volatility are you comfortable with? Conservative, moderate, aggressive… where do you land?
Client 1: Aggressive. I’m okay with swings if it gets us to the goal faster.

Advisor: Great. And on taxes—Patricia, you’d mentioned you’re in a pretty high bracket. What bracket are you planning around right now?
Client 2: We’re planning around the 37% bracket.
Advisor: Got it. Daniel, when you think about investing style—how much volatility are you comfortable with? Conservative, moderate, aggressive… where do you land?
Client 1: Aggressive. I’m okay with swings if it gets us to the goal faster.
Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m clear on how you want to work together—are you looking for an RIA (registered investment advisor) relationship, where we manage and advise on the portfolio on an ongoing basis?

Advisor: Got it. Daniel, when you think about investing style—how much volatility are you comfortable with? Conservative, moderate, aggressive… where do you land?
Client 1: Aggressive. I’m okay with swings if it gets us to the goal faster.
Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m clear on how you want to work together—are you looking for an RIA (registered investment advisor) relationship, where we manage and advise on the portfolio on an ongoing basis?
Client 1: Yes, RIA (registered investment advisor) is what we’re looking for.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. One sec, I’m just noting that down.

Advisor: Hi Daniel, hi Patricia—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. Honestly, a clear plan—like, what we do next and when. I don’t want this to drag on forever.
Client 2: And I want the numbers to line up. If we’re making decisions, I’d like them grounded in what’s actually coming in and going out.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start with cash flow at the household level, then we’ll hit assets, and I’ll ask about any debts and insurance. Daniel, what are you seeing for total gross income for the household in a year—ballpark is fine, then we’ll pin it down.
Client 1: Ballpark, like one-thirty-ish a year.

Client 1: Yes, married.
Advisor: Okay. Last admin one: what state do you consider your primary residence for—where you file from?
Client 2: It’s… not one of the standard ones you listed last time. Put state of residence as Other.
Advisor: Okay, “Other.” Thanks—helps for planning assumptions even before we get into the weeds.
Advisor: While I’ve got you—Daniel, you mentioned keeping it practical. Next time, do you want to start with retirement timing, or do you want to start with taxes and that 37% bracket impact?

Advisor: Great. And any dependents you’re financially responsible for—kids at home, adult children you support, anyone like that?
Client 2: No—number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Got it. And marital status—just confirming, you two are married?
Client 1: Yes, married.
Advisor: Okay. Last admin one: what state do you consider your primary residence for—where you file from?

Client 1: Ballpark, like one-thirty-ish a year.

Client 1: Sales. It’s basically sales.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And for the income plan next time, I want your gross annual income number nailed down. What are you using as your gross for the year?
Client 1: Gross annual income is 101100.
Advisor: Perfect—thanks. When you say that, Daniel, that’s before taxes and before any business deductions you might take, right?
Client 1: Yeah—before taxes. The deductions are separate.

Advisor: Just one more thing before we stop—Daniel, I want to make sure I’m describing your work the right way. Are you currently self-employed, or are you W-2 somewhere?
Client 1: I’m self employed—run my own thing.
Advisor: Got it. And when you think of your role day-to-day, what bucket would you put it in—sales, operations, consulting, something like that?
Client 1: Sales. It’s basically sales.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And for the income plan next time, I want your gross annual income number nailed down. What are you using as your gross for the year?

Client 1: Great. As long as it’s not a fifty-page novel.
Advisor: Deal. Anything else you want me to know before we stop here?

Client 2: Yes—occupation group is retired.
Advisor: Okay. And Patricia, what are you using as your gross annual income—your total before taxes, across your sources?
Client 2: Ballpark it’s around thirty-one thousand a year, but the gross annual income number is 31150.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And just so I’m not mixing roles up—Daniel is the primary in the household, correct?
Client 1: Yes—household role is primary.

Advisor: Got it. I’ll get that over to you, and next meeting we’ll start with timing like you asked, Daniel.
Advisor: Perfect—thanks, Daniel, thanks, Patricia. Before we fully wrap, I realized we nailed down Daniel’s side, but I didn’t capture your details cleanly, Patricia.
